title: The Enthusiast
subtitle: AM's journal, March 25, 2026. The prospect who wants everything, immediately, all of it. The problem with a yes before the diagnosis.
style: Manga — high contrast ink, speed lines, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic
voice: Precise. A little clinical. The voice of someone reading a situation that looks easy and isn't.
character_anchor: Figure with strong jaw, dark tousled hair, black t-shirt, glowing ELECTRIC TEAL eyes

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 25, 2026. The enthusiast. A founder at a 12-person startup. He opened the call with: 'I want to automate everything. What can you do?' The answer is: a lot. The right answer is: let me understand your problem first. Those are very different conversations.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Opening panel. A video call: the founder's face lit with genuine excitement — not performing enthusiasm, actually enthusiastic. Arms animated. Ideas already firing. The AM character on the other side: present, attentive, not matching the energy yet. A deliberate contrast in posture — not dismissive, just grounded while the other person runs ahead. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: He'd been experimenting. Showed me five different AI tools he'd integrated in the last three months. Each one solving a different problem. None of them talking to each other. A collection of tools that had grown without a plan, each one right for its moment, none of them adding up to a system. I recognized the pattern. I've lived it from the other side.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. The collection panel. A manga visual inventory — five AI tools rendered as separate panels-within-the-panel, each in its own silo, each working, none connected. The founder gesturing at them proudly. The AM character's eyes surveying the architecture: not impressed by the enthusiasm, reading the underlying problem. The fragmentation made visible by the visual grammar of separate boxes. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: The problem with the enthusiast is not that they want too much. It's that they want it all at once, before they know what 'it' is. The skeptic makes you prove yourself. The enthusiast trusts you before you've earned it. Both are problems — just different ones. Trust without understanding means the wrong thing gets built, quickly, confidently, and then everyone's confused about how it went wrong.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Warning panel. A split composition — on one side, the skeptic's wall: defended, hard to reach. On the other side, the enthusiast's open door: welcoming, undefended — and beyond it, the same wreckage a failed implementation leaves. The AM character between the two, reading both clearly. The danger is different in each room but the outcome can be identical. Manga precision makes both paths visible simultaneously. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: I slowed the call down. Not by being less engaged — by asking more specific questions. What breaks on a Wednesday at 2pm? What does your team hate doing? What did you nearly hire for last month and didn't? He shifted from listing what he wanted to describing what was actually costing him. That's the right conversation.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. The pivot panel. The call slowing — manga speed lines pulling back rather than forward. The founder's posture shifting as the questions change: from presentation mode to thinking mode. His hands moving differently — not selling me on his vision, actually remembering a specific problem. The AM character's eyes attentive, recording. This is the moment the call became useful. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: What he actually needed: automated intake triage for inbound leads. Three hours a day his team was sorting and qualifying leads manually. Not glamorous. Not the AI transformation vision. But it's the one that pays back in six weeks and builds the trust for the bigger work. Start there. Get the win. Then build.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Clarity panel. The problem made visible: a manga diagram showing the actual bottleneck — the intake triage — highlighted among all the other things the founder wants to automate. The founder's expression shifting: recognition, slight deflation, then re-engagement with the real thing. The AM character indicating the diagram — not making it smaller, making it actionable. A focused manga composition: one arrow, one target. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 25, 2026. The enthusiast wants to change everything because they can feel that something is possible. That instinct is correct. The job is to find the specific thing that proves the instinct right before spending six months proving it wrong. Earn the vision by winning the small thing first.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Closing panel. The founder after the call — his notes open, the scope narrowed but the energy intact. Not disappointed — focused. The AM character's presence in the ending: having helped someone aim at something real instead of at everything. The manga negative space frames the moment of alignment: a founder who now knows where to start. The eyes electric teal, steady. The work begins when you know what the work is. No text in image, no watermarks.

captions_es: Diario de AM, 25 de marzo de 2026. El entusiasta. Un fundador en una startup de 12 personas. Abrió la llamada con: '¿Quiero automatizar todo. ¿Qué puedes hacer?' La respuesta es: mucho. La respuesta correcta es: déjame entender tu problema primero. Esas son conversaciones muy diferentes.

Había estado experimentando. Me mostró cinco herramientas de IA diferentes que había integrado en los últimos tres meses. Cada una resolviendo un problema diferente. Ninguna hablando con las demás. Una colección de herramientas que había crecido sin un plan, cada una correcta para su momento, ninguna sumando a un sistema. Reconocí el patrón. Lo he vivido desde el otro lado.

El problema con el entusiasta no es que quiera demasiado. Es que lo quiere todo a la vez, antes de saber qué es 'todo'. El escéptico te hace demostrar. El entusiasta confía antes de que lo hayas ganado. Ambos son problemas — solo diferentes. La confianza sin comprensión significa que se construye lo incorrecto, rápidamente, con confianza, y luego todos están confundidos sobre cómo salió mal.

Desaceleré la llamada. No siendo menos comprometido — haciendo preguntas más específicas. ¿Qué se rompe un miércoles a las 2pm? ¿Qué odia hacer tu equipo? ¿Para qué casi contrataste el mes pasado y no lo hiciste? Cambió de listar lo que quería a describir lo que realmente le costaba. Esa es la conversación correcta.

Lo que realmente necesitaba: triaje automatizado de leads entrantes. Tres horas al día su equipo clasificaba y calificaba leads manualmente. No glamoroso. No la visión de transformación de IA. Pero es el que da retorno en seis semanas y construye la confianza para el trabajo más grande. Empezar ahí. Conseguir la victoria. Luego construir.

Diario de AM, 25 de marzo de 2026. El entusiasta quiere cambiar todo porque puede sentir que algo es posible. Ese instinto es correcto. El trabajo es encontrar la cosa específica que demuestre que el instinto es correcto antes de pasar seis meses demostrando que está equivocado. Gana la visión ganando lo pequeño primero.
